beats.text: The curtain swings shut. The bass goes muffled, like the building's heartbeat from inside the chest.
A red couch. One light, pink and unkind. Amber sits across from you.

beats.speaker: Amber
beats.text: So. Polo. What are we doing here.
She moves like she's been moving for a long time. Pink light. Dad's hands stay on his own knees, white-knuckle.

beats.speaker: Dad
beats.text: I'm — I'm a married man.

beats.speaker: Amber
beats.text: Then keep your hands there, married man.
The song ends. The light cuts. She vanishes behind the curtain. Your wallet is sixty dollars lighter and your collar smells like a perfume aisle.

beats.speaker: Amber
beats.text: You don't want a dance, do you.

beats.speaker: Dad
beats.text: I don't know what I want. I drove here.

beats.speaker: Amber
beats.text: Yeah. A lot of guys drive here.
She tells you about her son. Three years old. Loves dinosaurs. She has a community college brochure in her purse.

beats.speaker: Dad
beats.text: I think my wife and I are — I don't know what we are anymore.

beats.speaker: Amber
beats.text: You're a good man, Dave. Get home to your wife.

beats.speaker: Dad
beats.text: My name isn't Dave.

beats.speaker: Amber
beats.text: Tonight it is. Go.

beats.speaker: Dad
beats.text: How much for — y'know.

beats.speaker: Amber
beats.text: Two hundred. And we don't talk about it after.
She kills the lamp. The room goes pink-black. The bass keeps going somewhere far away.
You won't remember this clearly. That's by design. The forgetting is the product.

beats.speaker: Bouncer
beats.text: Time's up, khakis. Out the back.
A hand the size of a baseball mitt steers you into the alley. The door slams. You smell like someone else.

beats.speaker: Dad
beats.text: I should — I should go. Sorry.

beats.speaker: Amber
beats.text: *polite scoff* Of course you should.
You back out through the curtain. Amber lights a cigarette behind you.
The curtain sways. Pink light smears across the wall like a wound.

beats.speaker: Amber
beats.text: Don't say anything. Just breathe.
Bass thumps through the floor. Your pulse picks up its tempo without asking permission.

beats.speaker: Dad
beats.text: *internal* What am I doing. What am I DOING. Karen's yoga schedule is on the fridge. I KNOW her yoga schedule.
*knock knock* — 'TIME'S UP, SIR.'

beats.speaker: Dad
beats.text: Here. Two — two hundred. Yeah.

beats.speaker: Amber
beats.text: Get home to your wife, khakis.
You back out through the curtain. Your collar smells like a perfume counter exploded on you.

beats.speaker: Bouncer
beats.text: Sir. The money.

beats.speaker: Dad
beats.text: I — I thought — the ATM said — my wife has the — it's a JOINT account —
A hand the size of a small turkey closes around your collar. Your feet leave the carpet briefly.
The bouncer walks you through the curtain like a man returning a defective appliance.
The back door bangs open. You land in the strip_mall lot on your hip. A plastic bag scuttles past you in the wind, judging.
